does: This paper proposes using Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) techniques during the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stage of LLM alignment, rather than only during the subsequent preference-learning stage. Instead of directly learning from human demonstrations via standard supervised learning, the method simultaneously builds both a reward model and policy model from SFT data. The authors demonstrate that this IRL-based approach produces more effective SFT algorithms that are computationally efficient and robust to low-quality demonstration data, extracting better alignment signals from the same human demonstration dataset used in conventional SFT.
connects: This work builds on the standard two-stage RLHF pipeline where supervised fine-tuning precedes preference-based reward learning. It responds to limitations in how the SFT stage utilizes human demonstration data—typically treated as simple behavioral cloning targets without extracting deeper preference information. The paper connects to broader research on inverse reinforcement learning for inferring reward functions from demonstrations, as well as recent work questioning whether reward models learned only from preference comparisons fully capture human intent. It also relates to ongoing efforts to make alignment more sample-efficient and robust, extending IRL techniques from robotics and classical RL into the modern LLM fine-tuning setting.
enables: This approach opens directions for more efficient alignment pipelines that extract richer signal from limited human feedback. By making SFT stages learn implicit preference models alongside policy learning, the work suggests potential pathways toward unified alignment frameworks where demonstration data and preference data are processed more synergistically. It could enable practitioners to work with noisier or less abundant human supervision, and points toward understanding what implicit values and preferences are encoded in human demonstrations versus explicit preference judgments. Future work might explore combining this IRL-based SFT with improved downstream RL steps, or applying similar principles to other foundation model alignment scenarios beyond text generation.
